title: How Transformer Attention Mirrors Neural Circuits in the Hippocampus
deck: Striking parallels between self-attention mechanisms and the place-cell geometry of memory consolidation suggest deep architectural convergence.
content:
The hippocampus has long been understood as the brain's indexing system — a fast-binding structure that ties together distributed cortical representations into coherent episodic memories. When I first read Vaswani et al.'s original Transformer paper, I couldn't shake the feeling that I'd seen this architecture somewhere before.

The key insight: both systems implement a form of content-addressable memory. In transformers, the attention score between a query Q and key K determines retrieval weight. In hippocampal circuitry, pattern completion in CA3 recurrent connections performs structurally identical operations — a query pattern activates the most similar stored pattern through a form of Hebbian overlap scoring.

This essay explores what this convergence might mean — for AI architecture design, for our models of memory consolidation, and for the broader question of whether intelligence is substrate-independent.

title: Sleep as Synaptic Pruning: The Homeostasis Hypothesis Revisited
deck: New evidence from calcium imaging during NREM sleep challenges our assumptions about what the sleeping brain is actually optimizing.
content:
The synaptic homeostasis hypothesis (SHY) proposes that wakefulness leads to net synaptic potentiation, and sleep reverses this through downscaling. It's an elegant theory — and increasingly, a contested one.

Recent two-photon calcium imaging in mouse primary visual cortex shows that slow-wave activity during NREM sleep does not uniformly downscale synapses. Instead, it selectively strengthens synapses that were recently involved in learning while weakening others. This isn't homeostasis — it's sculpting.

title: The Octopus as a Model for Distributed Cognition
deck: Two-thirds of an octopus's neurons live in its arms. What does that mean for how we define a mind?
content:
An octopus arm, severed from its body, will still reach for food. The arm 'decides' to grasp. This single fact should unsettle every assumption we hold about where cognition lives.

With ~500 million neurons distributed across eight semi-autonomous arms, the octopus challenges the centralized-brain model that vertebrate chauvinism has normalized. Each arm contains a ganglion capable of local sensorimotor integration — reflexive behavior, texture discrimination, even simple learning.

title: Using Reinforcement Learning to Navigate Protein Fitness Landscapes
deck: Treating protein engineering as a Markov decision process opens surprising doors — and reveals why wet-lab feedback loops matter more than bigger models.
content:
Protein fitness landscapes are deceptive. High-fitness sequences tend to cluster in sparse, non-contiguous regions of sequence space, separated by fitness valleys. Evolutionary search — and most ML approaches — struggle here.

The RL framing offers something different: an agent that learns to navigate the landscape via active, adaptive sampling. State = current sequence. Action = point mutation. Reward = experimental fitness readout.

title: Thinking at 5,000 Metres: Cognitive Effects of High Altitude
deck: A field researcher's notebook from Ladakh — what hypoxia does to memory, attention, and the sense of self.
content:
The Pangong Tso sits at 4,350m. By the time I reached it, I'd been at altitude for six days — enough time for acclimatization to begin but not complete. My notebook entries from those days are... strange.

Hypoxia impairs prefrontal function first. Working memory degrades measurably above 3,500m, and cognitive flexibility — the ability to switch between tasks — suffers disproportionately. What remains intact longer: procedural memory, emotional salience, spatial navigation.

title: Deliberate Practice for Research Scientists
deck: The 10,000-hour rule is a myth, but the underlying science of expertise acquisition has genuine implications for how you structure your learning.
content:
Ericsson never said 10,000 hours. What he showed is that expert performance requires deliberate practice — structured activity at the edge of your current ability, with immediate feedback and focused repetition of weak points.

For research scientists, the implications are counterintuitive. Reading papers is not deliberate practice. Attending seminars is not deliberate practice. Writing first drafts, receiving brutal peer review, and immediately revising — that's closer.

title: The Gut-Brain Axis: Psychobiotics and the Future of Mood Regulation
deck: The enteric nervous system contains 100 million neurons. New research suggests we've been treating depression from the wrong end.
content:
The vagus nerve is a bidirectional highway between gut and brain. About 80% of its fibres carry signals upward — from gut to brain — making the enteric nervous system less a subordinate and more a collaborator in mood regulation.

The psychobiotic hypothesis proposes that targeted manipulation of gut microbiota can produce measurable anxiolytic and antidepressant effects. The evidence is promising but heterogeneous.

title: Building a Personal Knowledge System with Spaced Repetition
deck: How I use Anki, Obsidian, and a custom tagging taxonomy to retain 90% of what I read across three research domains.
content:
The forgetting curve is brutal and democratic — it doesn't care how important the paper is. Without active retrieval practice, most of what you read disappears within a week. Spaced repetition is the only intervention with a solid evidence base for long-term retention.

My system has three layers: (1) Anki for factual and conceptual retrieval, (2) Obsidian for linked, elaborative notes, and (3) a weekly review ritual that forces synthesis across domains.
